explicit_text: I was born in Hiroshima Prefecture as the first son of a poor farmer. Life there was hard, especially during the Sino-Japanese War when taxes increased and common people struggled. I remember walking to Hiroshima city when I was about 7 or 8 years old to visit a neighbor wounded in the Russo-Japanese War. The countryside was hilly, and we mostly grew rice and wheat in the winter. My grandmother raised me there until I came to America at age 13.
implicit_text: I was born in a rural area in western Japan as the first son of a poor farmer. Life there was hard, especially during the war between China and Japan when taxes increased and common people struggled. I remember walking to the nearby city when I was about 7 or 8 years old to visit a neighbor wounded in the conflict with Russia. The countryside was hilly, and we mostly grew rice and wheat in the winter. My grandmother raised me there until I came to America at age 13.

explicit_text: My father went to Hawaii before I was born, around 1895 or earlier, as an immigrant to grow sugar beets under a three-year contract. After his contract ended, he returned to Japan and married my mother. Later, my uncle also went to Hawaii as a contract immigrant before coming to America. Hawaii was a common first destination for Japanese immigrants before moving on to the mainland United States.

explicit_text: After my father left Japan again, he went to Canada before coming to America. He left my mother and me behind in Japan when he went to Canada at age 42. This journey was part of the immigrant experience for many Japanese seeking better opportunities.

explicit_text: The Sino-Japanese War from 1894 to 1895 was a difficult time for farmers like my family in Japan. The government raised taxes to pay for armaments, which made life very hard for common people, though the rich landowners did well. This economic strain was one reason my father left Japan to seek work abroad. I remember hearing about the war's impact on our village and how it shaped our family's struggles.

explicit_text: When I was about 7 or 8 years old, I visited a neighbor who was wounded and staying in an Army hospital in Hiroshima due to the Russo-Japanese War. That memory stands out because I got separated from my grandmother on the way home and cried. The war was a significant event in our community, and many men went off to fight. It was a time when discipline was strict even in schools, preparing boys to become soldiers.

The city was a central point in my early life and memories.

explicit_text: When I left Japan to come to America in December 1910, there was no pier in Kobe, so we had to take a rowboat to board the ship. The ship was small, about 6,000 tons, and the two-week voyage was difficult for me because I was used to fresh air and fresh food. Kobe was the port from which I departed on my journey to the United States.
